1: An allusion: 'YHWH spoke to Moses, saying...' — the final section of Leviticus opens with the same divine speech formula as every other section. Christ is the greater mediator: 'Long ago, at many times and in many ways, God spoke to our fathers by the prophets, but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son' (Hebrews 1:1-2). The law given through Moses is the penultimate word; Christ is the ultimate and definitive Word of God.
2: An allusion: persons dedicated by a special vow to YHWH. The complete self-dedication of Jesus to the Father's will is the fulfillment of every human vow of dedication: 'My food is to do the will of him who sent me and to accomplish his work' (John 4:34). 'I have come down from heaven, not to do my own will but the will of him who sent me' (John 6:38). His entire earthly life was a vow of dedication to the Father fulfilled without defection.
3: An allusion: the valuation of a male person between 20-60 years at 50 shekels of silver. The temple's system of monetary valuation for dedicated persons points toward the ultimate valuation. Thirty pieces of silver — the price for which Judas sold Jesus (Matthew 26:15) — is the slave's legal price (Exodus 21:32) and the price Zechariah received as wages (Zechariah 11:12-13). The one who was without price was priced by human assessors. Matthew interprets this as fulfillment: 'They took the thirty pieces of silver, the price of him on whom a price had been set' (Matthew 27:9).
4: An allusion: the valuation of a woman 20-60 at 30 shekels. The graduated valuation system reflects the practical economics of temple service (men performed more labor-intensive tasks) without assigning moral worth. In Christ all are redeemed at the same price — his own blood: 'You were bought with a price' (1 Corinthians 6:20; Leviticus 7:23). The costly, uniform price of redemption overrides the graduated valuations of the dedication system.
5: An allusion: the valuations for persons 5-20 years (male 20, female 10 shekels). The age-graduated system acknowledges different capacities for service. Christ receives all ages: 'Let the children come to me, and do not hinder them, for to such belongs the kingdom of heaven' (Matthew 19:14). His redemption is not conditioned on capacity or age — all are equally valuable to the one who gave everything.
6: An allusion: persons 1 month to 5 years — even infants have a valuation in the dedication system. The very young are not outside the covenant's scope. Christ's own dedication to YHWH occurred at 40 days (Luke 2:22-24) — as an infant, he was brought to the temple under the system of Lev 12 and 27. The first temple presentation of the one who would replace the temple is embedded in this dedication law.
7: An allusion: persons 60 and above (male 15, female 10 shekels). The elderly receive a lower valuation in the labor-capacity system, but Christ honors the elderly: 'Honor your father and mother' (Matthew 15:4). Simeon and Anna, both elderly, were the ones who recognized the infant Jesus in the temple (Luke 2:25-38) — the aged witnesses to the one who transcends the value-hierarchy of the dedication law.
8: An allusion: 'But if someone is too poor to pay the valuation, then he shall be presented before the priest, and the priest shall value him; the priest shall value him according to what the vower can afford.' The graduated mercy of the system — no one is excluded from dedication by poverty. Christ's redemption is available to all regardless of economic status: 'The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor' (Luke 4:18). 'Come, everyone who thirsts, come to the waters; and he who has no money, come' (Isaiah 55:1).
9: An allusion: a dedicated animal 'shall be holy to YHWH' — it cannot be exchanged or substituted. Once given to YHWH, the animal is in a category of its own. Christ's sacrifice was a once-for-all event that cannot be replicated or exchanged: 'Nor was it to offer himself repeatedly, as the high priest enters the holy places every year with blood not his own, for then he would have had to suffer repeatedly since the foundation of the world. But as it is, he has appeared once for all at the end of the ages to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself' (Hebrews 9:25-26).
10: An allusion: 'He shall not exchange it or make a substitute for it, good for bad or bad for good; and if he does in fact substitute one animal for another, both it and the substitute shall be holy.' The non-exchangeability of the dedicated animal. Christ's sacrifice admits no substitute: there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved (Acts 4:12). The uniqueness and non-substitutability of the Levitical dedication points to the unrepeatable uniqueness of the cross.
11: An allusion: an unclean animal that cannot be offered may be brought before the priest for valuation and redemption. The system provides for the redemption of what cannot directly serve as an offering. Christ redeems what is 'unclean' — what cannot by itself approach YHWH: 'While we were still sinners, Christ died for us' (Romans 5:8). He does not require his people to be clean before he redeems them; he redeems the unclean.
12: An allusion: the priest values the unclean animal 'whether good or bad; as the priest values it, so it shall be.' The priestly assessment determines the redemption price. Christ as the great High Priest who both assesses and pays the redemption price himself — the unique situation where the priest is also the payment (Hebrews 9:11-14).
13: An allusion: if the owner wishes to redeem the dedicated unclean animal, he adds a fifth to the valuation. The 20% surcharge recurs throughout Leviticus for inadvertent violations and personal redemptions (Leviticus 5:16; Leviticus 6:5; Leviticus 22:14; Leviticus 27:15, 19, 31). The surcharge principle acknowledges that removing something from the holy sphere carries a cost beyond the mere assessed value. Christ's redemption cost not a surcharge but everything: 'You were bought with a price' — the price being not silver and gold but 'the precious blood of Christ' (1 Peter 1:18-19).
14: An allusion: a house dedicated to YHWH is assessed by the priest. The household sanctuary dimension of the dedication law. Christ as the one who makes every home a potential temple: 'If anyone loves me, he will keep my word, and my Father will love him, and we will come to him and make our home with him' (John 14:23). The divine presence that the dedicated house pointed toward comes in the new covenant to dwell in every believer's heart.
15: An allusion: the owner who redeems his dedicated house adds a fifth. The redemption cost more than the original. This anticipates what became true in the incarnation: the cost of reclaiming what was lost was higher than the original gift. Creation was given freely; redemption cost the Son's blood. Paul: 'He who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all, how will he not also with him graciously give us all things?' (Romans 8:32).
16: An allusion: the dedicated field's valuation is based on the crop yield and the remaining years until Jubilee. The Jubilee year is the theological backdrop for this whole section — the 50-year cycle when debts were cancelled and lands returned to original owners. Christ inaugurated the true Jubilee: 'The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor... to proclaim the year of the Lord's favor' (Luke 4:18-19, citing Isaiah 61:1-2). The 'year of the Lord's favor' is the Jubilee vocabulary applied to the Messianic age.
17: An allusion: a field dedicated from the Jubilee year gets the full valuation. The proximity to Jubilee determines the value — the closer to the year of release, the higher the value. In Christ the Jubilee has arrived permanently: the release is not 50 years hence but already present. 'Now is the acceptable time; behold, now is the day of salvation' (2 Corinthians 6:2). The Levitical field-valuation tied to temporal Jubilee points to the timeless Jubilee of redemption.
18: An allusion: a field dedicated after the Jubilee year is valued proportionally to the remaining years. The diminishing value as Jubilee recedes mirrors the way the old covenant system was always running down toward its fulfillment-point. Each year further from the last Jubilee meant more years of servitude remaining. Christ's coming was the final Jubilee that need never run down: his release is permanent.
19: An allusion: the owner who redeems his dedicated field adds a fifth to the valuation. The redemption surcharge applies here as in the house (Leviticus 27:15). The costliness of redemption is a consistent theme: bringing something back from the divine sphere into ordinary use always costs more than it was originally worth. This anticipates the costliness of Christ's work — redemption from YHWH's ownership back to human possession required the supreme payment.
20: An allusion: if the field is sold to another person before being redeemed, it cannot be redeemed. The point of no return in the human system. Christ's redemption knows no such limit: 'And they are justified by his grace as a gift, through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus' (Romans 3:24). No human debt-situation is so hopelessly transferred that Christ cannot redeem — he has already paid the price for all who will come to him.
21: An allusion: a field that goes unredeemed at Jubilee becomes cherem (devoted, irrevocably consecrated) to YHWH — priestly property. The cherem category appears here as the ultimate form of divine ownership. Christ, in his resurrection, entered fully into divine possession — 'he was raised from the dead and will never die again; death no longer has dominion over him' (Romans 6:9). What is given entirely to YHWH and not redeemed becomes permanently his; Christ's resurrection body belongs permanently to the divine life.
22: An allusion: a purchased field (not ancestral) dedicated to YHWH gets its own valuation based on the Jubilee. The non-ancestral field dedicated by its purchaser — this is the situation of Gentiles brought into the covenant through Christ. The Gentiles are not the ancestral inheritance of the covenant people, but they have been 'purchased' (1 Corinthians 6:20; Revelation 5:9) and dedicated to YHWH through Christ. Even the field that was not originally 'yours' can be dedicated to YHWH.
23: An allusion: the priest calculates the valuation up to the Jubilee year, and the dedicated person pays that sum as holy to YHWH. The precise accounting of the dedication system contrasts with the comprehensive sweep of Christ's redemption: 'He gave himself as a ransom for all' (1 Timothy 2:6). No ledger-calculation is required in the new covenant; the debt was paid in full at the cross.
24: An allusion: at Jubilee, the field returns to its original owner. The Jubilee-return to original ownership points to the eschatological restoration: all of creation returning to its Creator's full possession. 'The creation itself will be set free from its bondage to corruption and obtain the freedom of the glory of the children of God' (Romans 8:21). The Jubilee's land-return is the natural type of the new creation's total restoration.
25: An allusion: 'Every valuation shall be according to the shekel of the sanctuary, twenty gerahs to the shekel.' The sanctuary shekel is the standard — the holy measure defines value. Christ as the standard by which all spiritual worth is measured: 'In him the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily' (Colossians 2:9). Every spiritual valuation that matters is calibrated to the one who is the fullness of God, the holy standard made flesh.
26: A direct type: 'But a firstborn of animals, which as a firstborn belongs to YHWH, no man may dedicate; whether ox or sheep, it is YHWH's.' The firstborn belongs to YHWH by right — it needs no special dedication because it is already his. Christ as 'the firstborn of all creation' (Colossians 1:15) and 'the firstborn from the dead' (Colossians 1:18; Revelation 1:5): he belongs to YHWH as no creature does, not by dedication but by nature. The firstborn law of Leviticus points to the eternal Son who is the Father's own, the firstborn who precedes and exceeds every created firstborn.
27: An allusion: an unclean animal's firstborn may be redeemed at valuation plus a fifth, or sold. The unclean-firstborn redemption is what Christ accomplishes for humanity: 'You were bought with a price' (1 Corinthians 7:23). Humanity, the 'firstborn' of the image-bearing creatures (Genesis 1:26-28), had become unclean through the fall. Christ redeems the unclean firstborn at the ultimate price — not a fifth surcharge but his own life.
28: An allusion: 'But no devoted thing (cherem) that a man devotes to YHWH, of anything that he has, whether man or beast or inherited field, shall be sold or redeemed; every devoted thing is most holy to YHWH.' The cherem — the irrevocably devoted — cannot be redeemed. This is the most absolute category in the dedication system. Yet Christ crosses even this boundary: what was given over to destruction (the condemned, the totally cursed) is what he came to redeem (Galatians 3:13: 'redeemed us from the curse of the law'). No depth of devotion-to-destruction is beyond his reach.
29: An allusion: 'No one devoted (cherem) who is to be devoted from mankind shall be ransomed; he shall surely be put to death.' The cherem person receives the death penalty — no ransom is possible. Christ took this unredeemable category upon himself: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us' (Galatians 3:13). He entered the unredeemable category, bore the death it required, and in doing so ransomed those who were under condemnation from which no human ransom was possible.
30: An allusion: 'Every tithe of the land, whether of the seed of the land or of the fruit of the trees, is YHWH's; it is holy to YHWH.' The tithe principle — the tenth belongs to YHWH, signifying that all belongs to him. Christ as the true firstfruits and tithe: 'Christ has been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep' (1 Corinthians 15:20). He is the first tenth given to YHWH, guaranteeing that the whole harvest follows.
31: An allusion: if a person redeems any of their tithe, they must add a fifth. The surcharge for reclaiming what was dedicated to YHWH — a pattern consistent throughout Leviticus 27. Giving to YHWH and then taking back costs more than simply giving. The principle of not drawing back from dedication to God: 'No one who puts his hand to the plow and looks back is fit for the kingdom of God' (Luke 9:62).
32: An allusion: 'And every tithe of herds and flocks, every tenth animal of all that pass under the herdsman's staff, shall be holy to YHWH.' The tithe of animals counted by the herdsman — every tenth. The Good Shepherd image: Christ is the shepherd who counts each sheep (Matthew 18:12-13; Luke 15:4-7). In a striking reversal, the one who owns all the flocks does not demand the tenth — he gives himself for every last sheep, including the lost one that is not yet in the fold.
33: An allusion: 'One shall not differentiate between good and bad, neither shall he make a substitute for it; and if he does substitute for it, then both it and the substitute shall be holy; it shall not be redeemed.' The absolute non-substitutability of the tithe. Christ's death as the non-substitutable sacrifice: 'There is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved' (Acts 4:12). No substitution is possible in the economy of salvation — it is Christ or nothing.
34: A direct fulfillment: 'These are the commandments that YHWH commanded Moses for the people of Israel on Mount Sinai.' The closing verse of Leviticus — the entire book, with all its sacrifice regulations, purity laws, festival calendar, holiness code, blessings and curses, and dedication laws, is summarized in one sentence: received on Sinai, mediated by Moses, intended for Israel. Christ fulfills each element: he is the sacrifice (Heb 9-10), the purification (Hebrews 9:14), the festival's fulfillment (1 Corinthians 5:7; Leviticus 15:23; Acts 2), the Holiness Code's embodiment ('be holy for I am holy,' 1 Peter 1:16), the one who bore the curse (Galatians 3:13) and secured the blessing (Galatians 3:14), and the one fully dedicated to YHWH (John 17:19). 'The law was given through Moses; grace and truth came through Jesus Christ' (John 1:17).
